summary:
Defective Rb gene phosphorylation disrupts normal cellular control mechanisms leading to unrestricted cell growth. This failure allows for excessive activation of E2Fs promoting rapid cell cycles associated with cancer development including conditions such as retinoblastoma. To summarize concisely while focusing only on critical aspects related directly to your query about what happens when there’s a defect in Rb gene phosphorylation, here are two core points highlighted above condensed into brief statements suitable for answering succinctly without extraneous details or tangential information not pertinent specifically addressing "what" occurs due to said deficiency rather than broader implications discussed previously regarding tumor suppression roles etc.: 

- Hypophosphorylated Rb cannot inhibit E2F activity
- Unchecked cells enter mitosis uncontrollably resulting potentially carcinogenically Increased rates of DNA replication & subsequent divisions occur freely lacking regulation normally imposed by functional Rb pathway components ensuring proper timing between phases within each complete round of interphase followed by M-phase transition during eukaryotic somatic nuclei' life-cycles across all This failure allows for excessive activation of E2Fs promoting rapid cell cycles associated with cancer development including conditions such as retinoblastoma.
